original_abstract: Identifying a maximum independent set is a fundamental NP-hard problem. This problem has several real-world applications and requires finding the largest possible set of vertices not adjacent to each other in an undirected graph. Over the past few years, branch-and-bound and branch-and-reduce algorithms have emerged as some of the most effective methods for solving the problem exactly. Specifically, the branch-and-reduce approach, which combines branch-and-bound principles with reduction rules, has proven particularly successful in tackling previously unmanageable real-world instances. This progress was largely made possible by the development of more effective reduction rules. Nevertheless, other key components that can impact the efficiency of these algorithms have not received the same level of interest. Among these is the branching strategy, which determines which vertex to branch on next. Until recently, the most widely used strategy was to choose the vertex of the highest degree. In this work, we present a graph neural network approach for selecting the next branching vertex. The intricate nature of current branch-and-bound solvers makes supervised and reinforcement learning difficult. Therefore, we use a population-based genetic algorithm to evolve the model’s parameters instead. Our proposed approach results in a speedup on 73% of the benchmark instances with a median speedup of 24%.
humanized_abstract: Maximal independent set The maximal independent set (MIS) problem is a classical NP-hard problem. It is used in several applications and the MIS problem asks to compute a maximum independent set, i.e., a set of vertices of a maximal size on an undirected graph where no two different vertices in the set are adjacent. In the last years, exact methods, particularly branch-and-bound and branch-and-reduce algorithms, are among the most performed solvers. Adding new or new formulations of valid inequalities in the bounding algorithm (branch-and-bound), in combination with efficient reduction rules within the search strategy (branch-and-reduce), have greatly improved the solution times on almost all known benchmarks. Thanks to the new reduction rules, even very large real world instances can be solved optimally that were beyond reach before. It remains open what the optimal branching strategy within the branch-and-bound and branch-and-reduce framework is, and we are about to investigate an embedding of a GNN that performs better. Currently, the state of the art is to choose at random vertices based on their degree in each iteration. We here show a GNN that learns to compute which vertex should be chosen as a next branching vertex. The solvers are rather complex, which exclude simple supervised or reinforcement learning. Hence, as a proxy, we evolve parameters for a population-based genetic algorithm, leading to improved solution times on 73% of our benchmarks with median speedup of 24%.
